title: RRHF: Rank Responses to Align Language Models with Human Feedback
abstract:
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) facilitates the alignment of large language models with human preferences, significantly enhancing the quality of interactions between humans and models. 
InstructGPT implements RLHF through several stages, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), reward model training, and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). 
However, PPO is sensitive to hyperparameters and requires multiple models in its standard implementation, making it hard to train and scale up to larger parameter counts.
In contrast, we propose a novel learning paradigm called RRHF, which scores sampled responses from different sources via a logarithm of conditional probabilities and learns to align these probabilities with human preferences through ranking loss.
RRHF can leverage sampled responses from various sources including the model responses from itself, other large language model responses, and human expert responses to learn to rank them.
RRHF only needs 1 to 2 models during tuning and can efficiently align language models with human preferences robustly without complex hyperparameter tuning. 
Additionally, RRHF can be considered an extension of SFT and reward model training while being simpler than PPO in terms of coding, model counts, and hyperparameters. 
We evaluate RRHF on the Helpful and Harmless dataset, demonstrating comparable alignment performance with PPO by reward model score and human labeling.
Extensive experiments show that the performance of RRHF is highly related to sampling quality which suggests RRHF is a best-of-$n$ learner.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper introduces a new learning paradigm called RRHF for aligning large language models with human preferences. The authors propose a ranking-based approach that leverages sampled responses from various sources to align the probabilities of generated responses with human preferences. They demonstrate that RRHF performs comparably to the state-of-the-art Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) method in terms of alignment performance. The experiments conducted on the Helpful and Harmless dataset show that the performance of RRHF is highly related to the sampling quality, suggesting that it is a best-of-n learner.

STRENGTHS:
1 The paper introduces a learning paradigm for aligning language models with human preferences, providing an alternative to existing methods like PPO.
2 The proposed algorithm is far more memory friendly because RRHF only needs to load one model at a time, while PPO needs to load 4 or 3 models (this depends on the implementation, TRLX requires 4 models but TRL requires 3).
3 The experiments conducted on the Helpful and Harmless dataset demonstrate that RRHF achieves comparable alignment performance with PPO.

WEAKNESSES:
1 Boosting the performance of LLMs by the generation from the model itself has been previously developed in instruction-tuning [1] and I think you may mention this line of works (see reference in [1]) in the related work for completeness. In comparison, I think the main advantage of this work is to apply this powerful idea in the alignment and also to use the reward model as a data filter (while the previous works mainly use the heuristics methods).

2 The online sampling-based RRHF is an interesting approach that combines classic data bootstrapping and online learning (RL). However, the results of online sampling are counterintuitive to me, as the updated model tends to generate meaningless responses, and further training on these samples leads to worse results. Intuitively, the updated models should be better than the initial checkpoint and generate better responses. I am wondering whether this is because the reward model used in the experiments is too weak (68.49%) or because the diversity of the generated samples is low. I think using a more powerful reward model like ChatGPT may help address this issue. Additionally, why does OP converge slower?

3 The reward model used in this paper is still weak, and overfitting the reward model may not necessarily offer advantages. This is evident in Appendix Table 2, where the aligned model achieves a high reward by losing diversity and always outputting "I highly recommend it to anyone." regardless of the prompt. I am wondering whether you have any specific method to decide whether to stop the training of RRHF to alleviate overfitting at some time. This is particularly important for the alignment with human preference because the evaluation is quite hard in this case.

4 It may be also possible to divide online sampling-RRHF into several stages to avoid loading multiple models at the same time (with a large K). This is because RRHF is an off-policy strategy and does not need to record the gradient along the line. I think this is the essential reason why PPO cannot load one model at a time.

5 The title is confusing. What is "without tears" referring to?  It is misleading and confusing for an academic paper.


[1] SELF-INSTRUCT: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions

QUESTIONS:
see weakness part

LIMITATIONS:
see weakness part
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes RRHF that ranks responses to align language model with human feedback. contributions include:
- algorithms to leverage various responses from multiple LMs to align with human preferences. drawing connection of viewing LM for both generation and a reward model for scoring.
- the engineering complexity of proposed approach is similar to finetuning and training a reward model, which is much less complex than the PPO approach for RLHF

STRENGTHS:
- Proposed a simple approach train LMs to rank responses from multiple LMs to learn from human feedback
- the approach is much more simple compared to PPO on implementation and computation, while having the competitive performance
- study on many sampling strategy, initial model checkpoints, and training settings (offline, iterative, online)

WEAKNESSES:
- description of different sampling strategy \rho 1-6 and the corresponding setting BP/DP... are really hard to follow. In general I feel the paper tries to combine too many ablations and studies without clearly explaining them, and this somehow dilute the core messages. 

- the section of categorizing RRHF as a "best-of-n Learner" is not fully supported by experiments. The experimental evidences are  avg/max reward of sampled policies has correlation with rewards of model's generated sequence. On the other hand, there is experimental results in Table 7 that have a ranking loss is very important. Without the ranking loss, learning from best of n is equivalent to distill from the best decode of n teachers. RRHF is "best-of-n Learning" is somewhat contradictory to contrastive learning is important. If it is not a core contribution of the paper, removing it might be better. If it is a core contribution of the paper, additional analysis such as removing rank losses for all settings in Table 5 and re-interpret the results based on that would help shine more lights on this topic.

QUESTIONS:
- \rho 1-4 and 5,6 from table 1 is confusing, there isn't explanations in this section. I can guess from other paragraphs they are different policies to sample decodes, but I do not understand their correlation with BP/DP/... 

- in table 5, PPO also learns from the proxy reward, how to justify that the accuracy is around random guess?

- in table 2, the "zero" setting combines models finetuned on hh dataset and non-finetuned models? it would be great to have separate annotations

- 3.1 "PPO must sample by its learned model \pi" is technically correct but all off-policy RL methods have the same properties.

LIMITATIONS:
NA
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper proposes RRHF, which adapts the BRIO (ranking loss) framework to the LLM alignment problem. RRHF can train a model to match with human rewards via a ranking loss defined by the margin between the normalized likelihood. Experiments show RRHF matches PPO's performance on the Anthropic's Helpful and Harmless dataset, while using simpler coding and hyperparameters. The authors also claims that this method combines the benefits of PPO and best-of-n sampling.

STRENGTHS:
1. The proposed RRHF method achieves comparable performance to PPO for aligning language models with human preferences on the Helpful and Harmless dataset, while being much simpler in terms of coding and hyperparameter tuning. This simplicity could make adoption easier.
2. RRHF only requires 1-2 models during training compared to 4 for PPO, which could make it more efficient and easier to tune.
3. RRHF extends the standard supervised fine-tuning approach and is similar to training a reward model (with the normalized log-likelihood as the reward), providing a smooth path from SFT to a full RLHF framework.

WEAKNESSES:
1. The authors claim RRHF is simpler than best-of-n sampling at inference time, but lack direct comparison. Since best-of-n also achieves good performance, an ablation study would help validate the advantages of RRHF.
2. I'm curious about the performance comparison to best-of-n sampling also because in some other research papers [1], it's reported that PPO is much more efficient than best-of-n. Since PPO has quite a few hyperparameters that needs to be tuned and the paper doesn't report how they tune the hyperparameters for PPO, it would be better if the proposed method can be compared to some already reported PPO results [2].
3. The core ranking loss used is adapted directly from prior work on contrastive learning on the summarization task, as well as the concept of scoring candidate responses based on log probabilities.
4. The performance is only evaluated on a single dataset designed specifically for alignment. Testing on broader tasks would better demonstrate generalizability.
5. The reward model's score is used as the main metric in the experiments (though there are some human evals in Table 3), which may lead to over-optimization in terms of the reward model.
6. RRHF needs a more advanced sampling strategy to work, like diverse beam search, while PPO can directly work with online sampling.

[1]: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback
[2]: Quark: Controllable Text Generation with Reinforced [Un]learning

QUESTIONS:
1. Some research [1] shows that RLHF with PPO can directly be used to finetune pre-trained models without SFT or context distillation. Is RRHF able to achieve that?

[1]: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback

Typos:

line 43: from the self model ==> from the model itself

LIMITATIONS:
This is an LLM paper, but no limitation is discussed
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a learning algorithm called RRHF. The idea is to produce an artifact similar to what Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) produces, except with a simpler learning algorithm (that might not require so many copies of the base model, e.g. for simultaneously the policy, reward, and reference models).

The proposed approach, Rank Responses to align Human Feedback ranks generations for a prompt via the reward model, and uses the rank (rather than the raw reward) for optimization.

More specifically, the approach obtains k sequences and the policy model computes the normalized log-probability of generating each one. If one sequence has a higher reward than another one, this approach says that it should have higher log-probability of generation. If so, there’s no ranking loss, and if not then the ranking loss is the difference of the normalized log-probabilities. There is also a supervised fine-tuning term.

The approach is tested by using a LLaMA LM on top of Anthropic’s Helpful and Harmless dataset of responses. Auto and human evaluation seem to suggest that this approach works well, outperforming Alpaca models trained with RLHF at the 7B parameter scale. The paper presents ablation studies over the sampling policy, checkpoints, and ranking loss.

STRENGTHS:
To this reviewer this paper seems strong:

* the idea of simplifying the RLHF stack seems important and helpful to the community, particularly getting rid of possibly unnecessary knobs and potentially using more off-policy data
* the experiments and ablation study seem well done, in particular comparing between LLaMA/Alpaca/Alpaca-sft

WEAKNESSES:
Though I am not an expert in this area, I’m not sure why the sampling policy during training doesn’t include random sampling and instead uses exclusively beam search style approaches. I think it would be a good thing to compare versus sampling approaches as these seem to be what PPO is doing.

Also, though I think overall the evaluation seems reasonable for this paper, an open question I have is whether this approach works better for surface level patterns like being friendly and helpful versus tougher logical reasoning. It might be helpful to show performance on logical reasoning tasks to see if they degrade mores than PPO.

QUESTIONS:
would it be possible to show performance on logical reasoning benchmarks?

What level of error/ stochasticity is around the head-to-head performance evaluations? I.e. if you sampled 20 responses with the same prompt, would it always be the case that RRHF+LLaMA would beat PPO+LLaMA? (or vice versa)

LIMITATIONS:
No. I think adding such a section would improve the paper (and I might increase my score if so / as long as the other questions are answered reasonably well, etc)
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This work introduces Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) to improve alignment between large language models and human preferences, enhancing interactions between humans and models. The proposed method, RRHF (Ranking-based Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback), efficiently aligns language models with human preferences without complex hyperparameter tuning. RRHF leverages sampled responses from various sources, learns to rank them, and shows comparable alignment performance with PPO on the Helpful and Harmless dataset, indicating it is a best-of-n learner.

STRENGTHS:
1. Compared to the reinforcement learning method PPO, RRHF is easier to implement and does not require a large amount of memory.
2. The paper's experiments are comprehensive, and the results of the ablation studies are also convincing.
3. The author introduced two modes, online and non-online sampling, providing valuable insights for the industrial application of the RRHF method.
4. RRHF only utilizes the reward model to rank responses instead of using the absolute value of the reward model. I believe this approach has more practical potential because using the absolute value of the reward model can be easily influenced by issues and may not be as robust.

WEAKNESSES:
1. RRHF requires utilizing a reward model to select the best answers rather than learning directly from human preference data. However, the reward model is typically not perfect, which poses a performance bottleneck for the RRHF method.
2. Recent work[1] observes the text degeneration problem (in Section 4 of reference [1]) when directly optimizing the L_{rank} loss.  How do you avoid this problem in your work?
3. The conclusion that "RRHF is a best-of-n learner" is currently based solely on experimental results, and it still lacks theoretical analysis and discussion.

Reference 
[1] Rafailov R, Sharma A, Mitchell E, et al. Direct preference optimization: Your language model is secretly a reward model[J]. arXiv preprint arXiv:2305.18290, 2023.

QUESTIONS:
Please see the Weaknesses.

LIMITATIONS:
Please see the Weaknesses.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper proposes another way to learn from feedback / preference data. They use the log probabilities of the candidates and try to train the model in a way that tries to increase the log probability of the chosen / better candidate and decrease the log probability of the non-chosen candidate.

They show that their approach does not require as much compute as RLHF and performs better on the Helpful and Harmless dataset.

STRENGTHS:
The paper presents a contrastive loss technique to learn from preference data.

The motivation is well justified though the paper lacks a comparison about computational resources as compared to RLHF. 

The results and ablations are sound.

The paper is generally well written and easy to follow.

The approach is relatively easy and straightforward and details to reproduce are provided.

WEAKNESSES:
The main contribution - contrastive loss (equation 2) - is exactly the same as the loss proposed in a work published last year at ICLR 2023 - https://openreview.net/pdf?id=0qSOodKmJaN and https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.00045 (equation 1). This work uses reward model to get the rank of the candidates, the prior work used embedding similarity / rouge scores which can be thought of as non NN reward models too. The only difference is the length normalization in the contrastive loss (divide the log probabilities by length in this work). Even using cross entropy to regularize is the same and explored. There is no mention or comparison related to that work and in light of knowledge about that, this paper does not seem novel at all.  For this reason I argue to reject the paper on grounds of lack of novelty. I'm happy to increase scores if this is address satisfactorily.

The paper lacks some comparisons which have been mentioned in the motivation (eg: motivates about efficiency gains as compared to RLHF but have no results supporting the claim).

It has been observed that many techniques like this just increase the reward score by generating longer responses. It would be good to compare the lengths of the responses from the different techniques.

QUESTIONS:
Can you compare with the prior work mentioned in more detail? It looks very similar.

Can you give a comparison of the response lengths of the different techniques? And provide length adjusted reward scores?

How many humans did you use for human eval? Can you provide more details?

LIMITATIONS:
NA
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The paper proposes a method to align LMs with a simple ranking loss. The reviewers appreciate the simplicity and effectiveness of this method. However, the major concerns are this paper's novelty and experiment setting. I suggest a weak accept for this paper based on the overall positive reviews.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
